## Introduction to the HVAC Industry’s Unique Landscape

The HVAC industry, a cornerstone of modern comfort, operates on a playing field distinct from other sectors. While many industries face their own set of challenges, those in the HVAC realm encounter a unique blend of demands and complexities. This article delves into these specific hurdles, offering a glimpse into the world of HVAC business owners.

## The Core Challenges Faced by HVAC Business Owners

At the heart of the HVAC industry are challenges that are as complex as the systems they manage. One significant hurdle is the intricate nature of HVAC technology. Unlike standard consumer products, HVAC systems require a deep understanding of mechanical and electronic components, making specialized knowledge and training paramount.

Moreover, HVAC businesses must navigate the ebb and flow of seasonal demand. The peak seasons bring a surge in workload, demanding rapid response and efficient service. This fluctuation requires astute business planning and resource management, a juggling act unfamiliar to many other industries.

Another layer of complexity comes from the regulatory landscape. HVAC systems are closely tied to environmental and energy regulations, which can vary widely by region and change frequently. Staying compliant while remaining competitive is a tightrope walk for many HVAC entrepreneurs.

## Navigating the Hurdles: Strategies for Success

Despite these challenges, success in the HVAC industry is not only possible but can be highly rewarding. One key strategy is investing in continuous training and development. Keeping abreast of the latest technological advancements and regulatory changes is crucial.

Owning your own HVAC Business is HARD!

Owning your own HVAC Business is HARD!

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Open your own shop, they said...

It'll be eeeeaaaaasy, they said...

You'll make tons of money, they said...

What "they" didn't tell you was that a lot, and I mean A LOT, more goes into being a successful Owner/Operator than being technically proficient.

You've got employees and their 99 problems.

You've got insurance and licenses to keep updated.

You've got customers that are literally impossible to satisfy and you spend more time in their attic than you do your own home.

You've got payroll to meet and overhead to take care of.

Not to mention your wife and kids might like to see you at least once a week.

You've got 80 things a week nipping at your time and attention and invariably things that are important get pushed into the, "I'll get to that tomorrow" pile that turns into the "Maybe next week" pile which then becomes the, "Oops. This has become a mess" pile.

Unfortunately, marketing often slips into that category and when the attention is paid to that area, it has become such a daunting beast that even beginning to attack the problem is slightly overwhelming and panic-inducing. If you are reading this maybe you are at that point or maybe you are just trying to prevent the mess from sneaking up on you. Either way, I'm going to share what you do to get this mess under control without driving yourself crazy.

1. Create a plan and stick to it. Mostly, anyway. A strong marketing plan is very important in keeping things moving, on schedule and on budget. I personally like to set a 6-month plan that is very detailed. I then project an additional 6 months with a plan that is less detailed. This gives me a 6-month roadmap and 12-month target that can/will be adjusted as organizational goals shift. Creating a 12-month plan that is detailed down to

This is the story of Shaun Dunn, founder of SD Plumbing & Heating in Edinburgh. From working seven days a week and nearly living out of his van, to building a £1.4M business now pushing towards £2.4M in 2026. But it did not start with structure or stability. In the early days, Shaun was stuck in the chaos so many trade business owners face. No proper systems. Constant cashflow pressure. At one point, over £100k sitting in unpaid invoices. Busy on site, but with no real control behind the scenes. Shaun built the company from nothing. Just long days, hard lessons, and the realisation that what gets you to £300k will not get you to £1M and what gets you to £1M will not get you to £2M. In this episode, he breaks down what actually has to change at each stage of growth. The mindset shifts needed to break the £1M ceiling, the operational fixes that remove the chaos, and what he wishes he had implemented far sooner. If you are wearing every hat, chasing payments, and feeling stretched despite growing revenue, this episode will resonate. A straight-talking masterclass for plumbing, heating, and home improvement business owners serious about building a structured, profitable multi-million pound company. 5 strategies I'd use to grow my plumbing business right now

5 strategies I'd use to grow my plumbing business right now

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

"Just another plumber with a wrench and a dream?"

4 years ago, that's what people thought when I talked about growing a plumbing business.

Well, I understand that.

It's easy to think plumbing is just about fixing leaks and unclogging drains.

But things have changed. Here's what I've learned:

1. A proper website is your 24/7 salesperson. It's not just an online business card. When done right, it's a customer-generating machine that works even when you're off the clock.
2. Google My Business is your local spotlight. Use local keywords like "Bob's Plumbing - Townsville Plumber" in your business name. Post updates weekly, snap a few photo's from a job and upload them with a bit of text. ALWAYS ask for reviews. If you're lazy, use a reputation management system to keep those 5-stars coming, cause those 1 star reviews will destroy any business.
3. Customer relationship management app. Not the fancy corporate stuff. Simple software for small businesses. All your customer data in one place. No more lost paper notes. Easier to manage jobs, your schedule and more time for actual money making.
4. Social media shows you're alive and kicking. Post 3 times a week minimum. Take quick videos on the job. Show the problem, the fix, maybe even the cost. This'll not only help you land more customers, but show that you're a live, ACTIVE business.
5. Never miss a call again. Set up automatic text responses. "Sorry, elbow-deep in pipes. Need a callback?" Customers feel valued, you don't lose customers. All on autopilot.

Cause here's the thing: If you don't work, you don't earn. But that's not the life you should live.

Your goal should be to build a business that runs without you always being on the tools. It'll take time, bu

As you grow, it’s easy to get caught up in acquisitions and big-picture strategies, but you can’t forget the operational basics. Call center management, dispatch, customer service—these are things that keep your business running day-to-day. When you skip over these steps to grow fast, you’re left scrambling to fix them later. And when you’re operating at a larger scale, those gaps can cause ripple effects throughout the business.
It’s like building a plane while you’re flying it. Growth isn’t smooth, and there will always be inefficiencies or processes you’ll need to refine. The upside to getting bigger, though, is that you have more resources to fix those issues

The Insurance Angle: How Metal Roofs Are Becoming Risk Management Tools

The Insurance Angle: How Metal Roofs Are Becoming Risk Management Tools

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

When a property owner in coastal Alabama can't get insurance until the roof meets FORTIFIED standards, that's not a roofing conversation anymore — that's a financial survival conversation. And contractors who understand the insurance angle are the ones getting the call.

Insurance has quietly moved from a background line item to a genuine dealbreaker. Homeowners and commercial building owners are getting non-renewed, absorbing double-digit premium hikes, or discovering they simply cannot find coverage at any price. The roof is at the center of that crisis. U.S. roof claims costs reached nearly $31 billion in 2024, up approximately 30% since 2022, and insurers have responded by scrutinizing roof condition, roof material, and roof rating in ways they never did a decade ago.

For contractors who want to grow their business in this environment, understanding the insurance landscape isn't optional background knowledge. It's a competitive advantage.

### How Bad Has the Insurance Market Gotten for Property Owners?

The home insurance market has tightened dramatically over the past three years, with premiums rising well above inflation, carriers exiting high-risk states, and non-renewal rates climbing in storm-prone ZIP codes. For contractors, these conditions mean clients who might have deferred a reroof are now being told by their insurer that deferring is no longer an option.

The numbers tell a clear story. The average cost of home insurance increased 8% in 2024— nearly triple the rate of inflation at 2.9% — and Insurify projects the average annual cost will reach $3,520 by end of 2025. IMPORTANT PSA!!!!!
The home insurance world is changing dramatically and changing FAST. Insurance companies are tired of paying for roof replacements and the significance of storms and their frequency have made every claim more difficult.
What are the BIGGEST changes?
1. Significant Premium increases - if things cost more(inflation), then the premiums to cover the risk has to increase.
2. Policy changes that are SIGNIFICANT and "hidden". Roof reduction schedules. Increased deductibles. Roof age automatic conversion AWAY from replacement(RCV) coverage to ACV coverage.
3. Drone and photo inspections followed by REQUIRED work or cancellation
4. Strict underwriting. If your roof is over 12 years old, you may not get coverage. If your home is over 50 years old, you may not get coverage.
I can tell you which carriers are good and which carriers are not. Since

# How a newspaper typo taught me about pricing and value

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

9mo

The most expensive lesson I didn’t have to pay for… Was a newspaper typo. When I first started my business, I didn’t overthink pricing. I did what a lot of scrappy entrepreneurs do: I looked at what everyone else was charging. I opened the classified section of the newspaper under "rubbish removal" and saw prices ranging from $80 to $120 per truckload. Easy enough. I figured, I’m going to be offering top-notch service, so I went straight for the high end: $120. My thinking was simple. I knew even back then that in the service industry, you get what you pay for and so do your customers. I wanted to train my team well, treat them right, and go the extra mile for every job. We didn’t just haul junk; we swept up after ourselves and made sure every customer walked away happy. That level of service takes time, effort, and great people - and that doesn’t come cheap! Now here’s where it gets interesting… One day, The Vancouver Province ran a piece on my business. It was a nice feature…except they accidentally printed our truckload price as $138 instead of $120. Almost a 14% increase!! At first, I thought, “Well, that’s going to scare people off.” But the exact opposite happened. The phone started ringing off the hook!! We had over 100 bookings come in right after that article ran. And it hit me: the market could actually handle a higher price, as long as people believed they were getting great service. That simple pricing mistake helped reposition the value of junk removal in people’s minds. And it taught me a power

Home Service Business Growth Stagnant Due to Ineffective Marketing | Phil Risher posted on the topic | LinkedIn

Home Service Business Growth Stagnant Due to Ineffective Marketing | Phil Risher posted on the topic | LinkedIn

Phil Risher

2w

The most dangerous number in a home service business is comfort. Last week a contractor told me they had worked with the same marketing company for ten years. Ten years. Revenue stayed flat at one point two million the entire time. No real evaluation. No hard questions. Just loyalty and hope. I see this constantly in home services. Owners work relentlessly in the business but rarely step back to question the systems driving growth. Marketing becomes a background expense instead of a strategic engine. Here is the uncomfortable truth. If your marketing has not evolved, your business probably has not either. Customer behavior changes. Search habits shift. Competition gets smarter. Costs rise. What worked five years ago is rarely enough today. What worked ten years ago is almost always holding you back. Strong marketing is not about flashy tactics or chasing trends. It is about alignment. Alignment between your growth goals and your lead flow. Alignment between customer expectations and your brand promise. Alignment between marketing and operations so demand does not outpace delivery or stall growth. I tell owners this all the time. Any serious marketing partnership should earn its keep every six months. Six months to see momentum. Six months to measure lead quality. Six months to assess execution on both sides. And yes, sometimes the issue is not the agency. Sometimes the business is not ready to support the strategy. Slow follow up. Undertrained staff. Capacity issues. Growth requires ownership at every level. The opportunity in home services right now is massive. But only for businesses willing to challenge comfort and build for the long term. If you feel stuck, ask yourself one question. Is my marketing designed for where I wan

Cash Flow Solutions for Service-Based Businesses: Keeping Your Finances Flowing

Cash Flow Solutions for Service-Based Businesses: Keeping Your Finances Flowing

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Maintaining positive cash flow can be one of the trickiest challenges for service-based businesses. Unlike product businesses, where customers pay up front, service companies often face delayed payments, fluctuating demands, and the complexities of covering recurring expenses with uneven revenue. Fortunately, with the right strategies, you can improve cash flow, manage financial unpredictability, and ensure your business is ready to thrive.

### Understanding the Cash Flow Challenge for Service-Based Businesses

According to a report by the U.S. Small Business Administration, over 80% of small businesses experience cash flow challenges, and service-based businesses are particularly vulnerable due to delayed billing cycles and irregular payment structures. This can be especially tough when you need to cover fixed expenses like rent, payroll, and utilities.

### Strategy 1: Set Up Predictable Payment Structures

Cash flow issues often arise due to inconsistent payment timelines. To combat this, establish predictable payment structures:

- Retainer Agreements: If you provide ongoing services, offering retainer agreements can provide reliable, regular payments. Retainers can help smooth out cash flow fluctuations by ensuring monthly income.
- Milestone Billing: For longer projects, split the total payment into milestones (e.g., at 25%, 50%, and 75% of project completion). According to research by FreshBooks, businesses that use milestone billing report a 30% improvement in cash flow stability.
- Upfront Deposits: Consider requiring an upfront deposit before starting work. This guarantees some cash flow at the start of each project and helps cover any immediate expenses.

>

7 Field Service Management Challenges (And How To Solve Them)

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By Edward Bell

## Introduction

Excellent field service management is where operational efficiency, customer satisfaction, and skilled resource management meet. But balancing these three factors is easier said than done, especially as the mobile workplace continues to transform in often unexpected and unpredictable ways.

In this blog, we’ll dive into the top challenges field service managers face and, more importantly, explore real-world solutions to these challenges. Whether you’re looking to refine your current processes or overhaul your strategy entirely, you’ll find insights here that could be the key to unlocking enhanced operational efficiency and customer loyalty.

---

## What is field service management?

As a quick refresher, what exactly do we mean by field service management (FSM)? At its core, FSM is about coordinating field operations through a mobile workforce. Field service management often includes a wide range of activities, from scheduling and dispatching to managing work orders and ensuring timely service delivery at customer sites.

> FSM is crucial for industries that involve repairs, installations, maintenance, or any service requiring a technician’s on-site presence. It encompasses everything you need to ensure that the right technician with the right skills and parts reaches the right location at the right time. And in today’s digital age, this isn’t just about human management—it’s also about smart integration of technology.

Field service management heavily relies on software and tools to automate and streamline tasks such as:

These Google Ads Mistakes Are Costing Home Service Businesses $10,000s

These Google Ads Mistakes Are Costing Home Service Businesses $10,000s

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Last month, I had the opportunity to speak with 25 home service business owners about their Google Ads strategies. What stood out to me was that 99% of them were making the same five mistakes. These errors were holding them back from getting better leads and maximizing their return on investment (ROI). In this post, I’ll walk you through each mistake and show you how to fix them so you can start seeing real results.

---

### Mistake #1: Not Knowing If Google Ads Are Actually Working

One of the most common questions we hear is, What should my budget be? How much should I spend? Should I spend more or less? If you’re not tracking your Google Ads performance in your CRM and pulling a return on ad spend (ROAS) report every month, making informed decisions becomes nearly impossible.

Why It Matters: Without proper tracking, you’re essentially throwing money at Google and hoping for leads. This approach rarely works.

How to Fix It:

- Integrate your Google Ads with a CRM that can track leads and revenue.
- Focus on collecting accurate data—better data leads to better decisions.
- Review your ROAS monthly to understand what’s working and what isn’t.

---

### Mistake #2: Using the Wrong Keywords and Ignoring Negative Keywords

Many home service business owners target broad keywords and hope Google will direct the right traffic to their website. Unfortunately, this strategy often leads to wasted ad spend on irrelevant traffic.

Why It Matters: Broad keywords can result in high costs per click, especially in competitive markets. Ignoring negative keywords, such as “cheap plumbers” or “cheap electricians,” means your ads might show up for searches you don’t want to pay for.

5mo

Most Roofers Think Google Ads Don’t Work… But It’s Not Google That’s the Problem Here’s the truth nobody tells trades businesses: Google Ads do work — you’ve just been taught the wrong way to use them. Let me break it down in the simplest way possible 👇 When someone’s roof starts leaking at 2am in Dublin, they grab their phone and type: “roofer near me” Your ad appears. They click. They land on your page. They call you. Simple… if your campaign is set up correctly. The real problems usually look like this: ❌ Broad match keywords draining your budget ❌ Sending everyone to a generic homepage ❌ No negative keywords ❌ No conversion tracking ❌ One campaign trying to sell every service ❌ Agencies owning your Google Ads account This is why roofers say, “Google Ads didn’t work for me.” Here’s what actually works: ✅ Exact & phrase match keywords ✅ Service-specific landing pages (roof repairs, gutters, chimneys, flat roofs…) ✅ Clear call, email & WhatsApp options ✅ Location-based headlines ✅ Proper conversion tracking ✅ A strong negative keyword list ✅ You owning the Google Ads account — not the agency When you run ads properly, the difference is night and day: More calls. More qualified leads. Lower cost per lead. Zero time wasted.
